demoted.reason: spurious 1-member antithesis set: n002 is already a rival in s1; demoted per lint (SPEC §3.2)

frictions.text: The lipid-mechanism grounds n005 (HDL/ratio-unchanged), n008 (sat-fat-not-cholesterol), and n009 (dietary-cholesterol-raises-LDL) all connect to the root CVD/mortality claims n002/n001 via plain Ground edges (e006, e007, e008), but every one of them is actually a surrogate-marker finding (see n016: lipid RCT endpoints are surrogates, not hard CV outcomes). The Ground relation has no way to mark that the inferential step from 'this diet moved a lipid marker' to 'this diet moves cardiovascular/mortality risk' is a qualitatively weaker, different kind of inference than a study that measured CVD events or mortality directly — it renders identically to a direct-evidence edge.

n014 (hyper-responder vs hypo-responder individual variation in cholesterol response) doesn't fit the Ground/Antithesis grammar cleanly: it isn't a rival positive claim to either root answer (it doesn't assert 'eggs are bad' or 'eggs are not bad'), and it isn't support for either side either — it's a moderator claim asserting the true answer differs by person, which undercuts the shared presupposition that 'most people' is a single uniform group both n001 and n002 assume. There's no relation in the grammar for 'this claim complicates the framing of the question rather than supporting or rivaling an answer,' so I left it un-grounded to either root rather than force a Ground edge in either direction.

The 2025 crossover trial (s05, source of n006/n007/n008's grounds) is a single small (n=61) short-term trial, while the RCT meta-analysis backing n004/n005 (s04) pools 28 trials. Both surface as ordinary Ground edges with identical formal status (e003, e004, e001, e002); the grammar has no structural way to encode that n008 currently rests on much thinner evidence than n004/n005 do, short of writing that caveat directly into the claim text (which I did in n006/n007's wording) — there's no evidence-weight/power dimension distinct from post-hoc agree-count strength.

The type-2-diabetes subgroup result (n038/DM_SUBGROUP_CVD, and its causal/confounded rivals n039/n040) is deliberately NOT grounded into the root claim n001 ('eggs are bad for most people'), because diabetics are a subgroup, not most people, and doing so would generalize a subgroup-scoped finding to the general population -- exactly the error the evidence does not support. But the grammar has no formal way to mark a node as SCOPE-RESTRICTED to a named subgroup as opposed to universally quantified; a node's text can say 'among people with type-2 diabetes' but nothing prevents a later author from drawing a plain Ground edge from n038/n039 straight to n001, and that edge would look identical -- structurally -- to any other Ground. There is no scope/quantifier dimension on the Ground relation, only on the prose inside the node.

Source s01's own listed claims are in internal tension: claim 1 (anchored as n022/EA_s01a) states the 41-cohort meta-analysis found egg/dietary-cholesterol consumption 'associated with increased overall and cardiovascular-disease mortality' in general, while claim 3 (anchored as n024/EA_s01c) states that, in dose-response terms, each 100 mg/day cholesterol increment was 'not clearly' associated with CVD mortality specifically. Both are faithfully anchored as separate external_anchor nodes from the same source id (s01), since neither is false and they likely reflect different exposure framings (composite egg+cholesterol vs. cholesterol dose-response) within one paper -- but the Ground/anchor grammar has no way to flag 'these two anchors come from the same paper and may need reconciling' as distinct from 'these are two independent corroborating findings.' A reader following both n022 and n024 into downstream claims (n033 and n032 respectively) cannot tell from graph structure alone that they might partially offset rather than accumulate.

Several of my independent Grounds for the same dependent likely draw on overlapping or correlated cohort populations rather than truly independent samples -- e.g. n017/EA_s09 (three large US cohorts + meta) and n022/EA_s01a (41-cohort meta) both draw heavily on the same large US cohort studies (the kind of cohorts that recur across egg-mortality meta-analyses), yet they ground different, rival downstream claims (n031/CGEN toward 'not bad' and n033/CVDMORT_POS toward 'bad') as if independent. The Ground relation has no mechanism to mark 'these two pieces of evidence share underlying subjects' as opposed to 'these are two independent replications' -- so the graph's apparent evidential weight (more independent Grounds = more support) can overstate true independent replication whenever meta-analyses pool overlapping cohorts, which is common in this literature.

Source s09's second listed claim -- that the three-US-cohort BMJ 2020 analysis 'is frequently cited as the strongest general-population evidence that eggs are not a meaningful CVD risk factor' -- is a claim about the study's RECEPTION in the literature (a sociological/citation fact), not a first-order finding about eggs and cardiovascular health. I chose not to anchor it as a Ground for n031/CGEN, because doing so would let a study's popularity substitute for its evidential content -- 'often cited' is not itself a reason eggs are or are not risky. The grammar has no separate relation for meta-evidential claims about a source's standing (as opposed to what the source found); the only options are to force it into an ordinary Ground (misleadingly) or drop it (losing a claim that is in the source pack). I dropped it and flag that as a gap the grammar doesn't cleanly resolve.

Recurring pattern: several Ground edges license a causal-sounding dependent from a purely associational (or non-associational) premise, with the grammar giving no way to mark that slippage on the edge itself. e.g. e016 grounds n031 ('egg consumption... is not associated with meaningfully increased CVD risk' -- an associational finding) into n002 ('does not meaningfully raise... risk' -- causal-effect language); e008 grounds n009 ('meaningfully raises serum LDL', itself a contested surrogate-marker claim) directly into n001 ('meaningfully raises... CVD and mortality risk', a clinical-endpoint causal claim) without the surrogate-to-clinical step being represented; e019/e029/e031 do the analogous move on the confounding side (n032/n035/n036 -> n002). A plain Ground edge reads identically whether the step from evidence to claim is definitional, associational, or causal -- the grammar has no typed-edge way to flag 'this inference crosses from association to causation' short of a free-text comment or a competing claim node (which is what n045/n046/n048 in this session do, but only for the aggregate 'is it settled' question, not edge-by-edge).

Correlated cohorts treated as independent lines of evidence: n017/EA_s09 (three large US prospective cohorts + updated meta-analysis, BMJ 2020), n020-021/EA_s02 (dose-response meta-analysis pooling ~55 studies, ~2.7M individuals), n022-024/EA_s01 (updated meta-analysis of ~41 prospective cohorts), and n025-026/EA_s10 (large US population-based cohort) are each anchored as if they were four separate bodies of evidence. In nutritional epidemiology, meta-analyses of this kind draw heavily on the same recurring US and European prospective cohorts (e.g. Nurses' Health Study, Health Professionals Follow-up Study, NHANES-linked cohorts, ARIC) -- a 41-study and a 55-study pooled meta-analysis on the same exposure-outcome pair almost certainly share a large fraction of underlying cohort data with each other and with the '3 large US cohorts' analysis, rather than constituting four independent replications. The graph's Ground/independent-vs-conjunction grammar has no way to express 'these N grounds are drawn from overlapping source populations, so their independent-support weight should be discounted below N' -- each is drawn as an ordinary independent Ground, which will read as stronger corroboration than the underlying data independence actually supports. This applies within the general-population strand (n017, n020-024, n025-026 all grounding n031/n032/n033/n034) and plausibly within the diabetes-subgroup strand (n027-030, s07/s08) too.

Surrogate endpoint standing in for a clinical one, at the edge level rather than the node level: n016 (RCT lipid markers are surrogates, not direct measurements of events/mortality) and n047/CLAIM_C now exist as content nodes naming this gap, but the Ground edges that actually DO the substitution -- e006 (n005 HDL/ratio-unchanged -> n002 'does not meaningfully raise... risk'), e007 (n008 sat-fat-drives-LDL -> n002), e008 (n009 raises-LDL -> n001 'raises... risk'), and e001/e002 (n003, the RCT lipid meta-analysis, -> n004/n005) -- carry no marker that they cross from a surrogate (LDL/HDL/TC) to a clinical (CVD events, mortality) endpoint. Adding n047 as an extra rival/ground elsewhere documents the gap in content but doesn't retroactively qualify e006/e007/e008/e001/e002 themselves; a reader following just the edges would see plain undifferentiated support all the way from lipid RCT to mortality-risk claim. This is the same shape as f8 (association-as-causation) but is specifically about endpoint TYPE (surrogate vs. clinical), which is a measurement-validity issue distinct from the observational-vs-causal issue f8 names.

Structural-fit question on n048 ('the causal question remains genuinely unresolved'), added as a third member of root antithesis set s1 alongside n001 ('eggs meaningfully raise risk') and n002 ('eggs do not meaningfully raise risk, and have benefits'). n048 is not a rival in the same sense as n001/n002 -- it isn't a competing directional claim about eggs, it's a meta-level claim about the state of the evidence, and it is not logically exclusive with either: someone could hold n001 (weakly) AND n048 (the evidence doesn't yet establish it) at once, whereas n001 and n002 as phrased are close to exclusive of each other. Antithesis-set membership reads all three the same way (rival positive claims competing for the same belonging weight), with no way to mark that n048 operates at a different level (epistemic-state claim) than n001/n002 (object-level claims about eggs). The alternative -- grounding n048 as a plain Ground into both n001 and n002 as a shared 'this is contested' qualifier -- felt equally arbitrary since n048 doesn't support either conclusion, it undercuts confidence in both. I chose antithesis membership per round_build.md's instruction to 'let the graph show' the causal question staying open as one of the three top-level distinctions, but flag that the choice between 'third rival' and 'shared undercutting qualifier with no home' was not principled by the grammar itself.

Two benefit claims in my cluster (n059 weight-management, n060 lutein/zeaxanthin-and-eye-disease) are grounded ONLY by s15, a consumer-health/blog source that the pack itself flags as secondary, with no primary-literature anchor available in the source pack to corroborate them. The grammar has no way to mark an external_anchor's SOURCE TIER (primary study vs. consumer-health summary) on the node itself -- that distinction currently lives only in prose inside the anchor's text and in a comment I added on the edges (c001, c002), not as a structural signal a reader would see without opening the thread. This is the same asymmetry method-03 and epi-02 likely hit from the risk side: source-quality variance needs a first-class place to live, not just node text and comments.

n049 (the plain fact that the 2015-2020 guidelines removed the 300mg/day cholesterol cap) is a Ground I drew into BOTH n061 ('removal reflected evidence-based reassessment') and n062 ('removal nonetheless left genuine scientific caution') -- the two rival readings of the same guideline event in antithesis set s8. n049 doesn't actually discriminate between these readings; it's a shared presupposed fact both interpretations need, not independent evidence for either one. Drawing it as a plain Ground edge into both makes it look like corroborating evidence for two opposed conclusions, when really it's the common ground of a framing/interpretive fork -- structurally the same issue as the hot-dog worked example in AGENT-GUIDE (both sides depend on a shared definitional fact, not further evidence).

n063 ('eggs' benefits outweigh their modest risk, reasonable part of a healthy diet') and its rival n064 ('precaution warranted') are value/ought-adjacent tradeoff claims resting on purely empirical grounds (nutrient-density, satiety, subgroup risk, open causal question). Per the AGENT-GUIDE is/ought nudge, an ought-leaning claim should have its value premise (e.g., 'accepting small/uncertain risk for concentrated nutrient/satiety benefit is a reasonable tradeoff' vs. 'when causal risk is unresolved, avoiding the exposure is the prudent default') made an explicit Ground -- but the grammar doesn't force this, and adding a standalone 'this tradeoff principle is worthwhile' node felt like manufacturing content the sources never asserted rather than extracting it. Left the value premises implicit in the claim text instead; flagging rather than forcing.

Magnitude/quantifier slippage on e005: n009 ('dietary cholesterol MEANINGFULLY raises serum LDL for most people' -- the sole Ground for the 'cholesterol-bad' antithesis member in the LDL-driver crux s3, and the only node directly grounding the root n001 on the lipid side via e008) is grounded by exactly one edge, e005, from n004, which itself asserts the RCT-measured rise is 'only MODEST.' The Ground relation has no way to flag that the dependent's quantifier ('meaningfully') is stronger than the premise's own quantifier ('modestly') licenses -- the edge renders identically to any well-calibrated Ground. This is distinct from f8/f10 (association-as-causation, surrogate-vs-clinical): here both n004 and n009 are surrogate-marker claims at the same evidential level, and the slippage is purely in DEGREE language, not evidence type. Net effect: the weakest-evidenced member of the LDL crux (n009, feeding the 'eggs are bad' root) looks structurally as well-supported as any other single-Ground claim in the graph.

Friction reconciliation (synth-05 pass): f1, f3, f8, and f10 (surrogate-vs-clinical endpoint, evidence-weight asymmetry, association-stated-as-causation) and f6/f9 (correlated/overlapping cohorts treated as independent) are, underneath their different framings, the SAME single grammar gap wearing two costumes: a plain Ground edge carries no evidence-TYPE metadata (surrogate marker vs. clinical/hard endpoint; associational vs. interventional design) and no evidence-INDEPENDENCE metadata (which underlying cohort/subject pool a finding was drawn from). Concretely: e001/e002/e003/e004 (RCT lipid meta-analyses -> n004/n005/n008) and e005/e006/e007/e008 (those surrogate-marker claims -> the CVD/mortality root claims n001/n002) all cross the surrogate/clinical line invisibly; and n017 (BMJ 2020, 3 US cohorts + meta), n020-021 (55-study/2.7M dose-response meta), n022-024 (41-cohort meta), and n025-026 (US population cohort) all ground rival downstream claims (n031 vs n032/n033/n034) as if fully independent samples, when nutritional-epidemiology meta-analyses of this exposure-outcome pair routinely re-pool the same recurring US/European cohorts (Nurses' Health Study, Health Professionals Follow-up, NHANES-linked, ARIC). Six separate friction flags across three authors point at one missing dimension on the Ground relation -- not six distinct grammar failures. Recorded once here per round_build.md's friction-reconciliation duty; the individual flags are left in place (append-only) since each documents a distinct concrete instance worth keeping, but a reader of the falsification log should treat f1/f3/f6/f8/f9/f10 as instances of this one structural gap.

n063 bundles an is-evaluation ('benefits outweigh the modest/contested risk') with an ought ('a reasonable part of a healthy diet'). Per SPEC 1.3 the ought should be split out and grounded on the is plus an explicit value premise. Deferred: the value-premise ought can only be wired once n063/n064 are retyped to ought (Hume rule forbids an ought grounding a claim-typed node), which is the categorical-consensus poll's job.

demoted.reason: ghost-eligible (own Agreement 1.13, below floor); self-contradicting chain: ground n004 says LDL rise is only modest, dependent n009 says meaningfully (SPEC 3.1/3.2)

comments.text: Ground n056 is s15, a consumer-health (Healthline) source, not a primary study -- the pack itself flags this and notes individual mechanistic-benefit claims from it should be treated as secondary. The protein-content figure (~6g/egg) is uncontroversial nutrition-facts data, but the satiety/weight-management inference deserves lower weight than the primary-literature anchors elsewhere in the graph.

Ground n057 is also s15 (consumer-health/blog). Unlike the nutrient-density claims anchored to s14 (a primary study), this specific mechanistic claim -- lutein/zeaxanthin reducing cataract/macular-degeneration risk -- has no primary-literature anchor in this pack. Treat n060 as the weakest-anchored node in my cluster; it should score for what a blog says, not for what the ophthalmology literature actually shows.

meta.question: Are eggs bad for your health? (Does egg / dietary-cholesterol consumption meaningfully raise cardiovascular-disease and mortality risk for most people?)

phrasings.text: Egg and dietary-cholesterol consumption meaningfully raises cardiovascular-disease and mortality risk for most people.

titles.text: Eggs are bad for your health (meaningful CVD/mortality risk)

phrasings.text: Egg and dietary-cholesterol consumption does not meaningfully raise cardiovascular-disease risk for most people, and eggs provide real nutritional benefits.
On balance, for most people eggs are not bad for health: they do not meaningfully raise cardiovascular-disease risk, and they provide real nutritional benefits.

titles.text: Eggs are not bad for your health (negligible CVD risk, real benefits)

phrasings.text: A meta-analysis of 28 randomized controlled trials found egg consumption raised total cholesterol by about 5.6 mg/dL, LDL-C by about 5.55 mg/dL, and HDL-C by about 2.13 mg/dL relative to low-egg control diets.

source.title: Association between Egg Consumption and Cholesterol Concentration: meta-analysis of RCTs (PMC; PubMed 32635569)

titles.text: RCT meta-analysis: egg effect on TC/LDL/HDL (s04)

phrasings.text: Egg consumption raises LDL cholesterol only modestly in randomized-controlled-trial evidence.

titles.text: Modest LDL rise from eggs (RCT evidence)

phrasings.text: Egg consumption raises HDL cholesterol alongside LDL, leaving cholesterol ratios (LDL:HDL, TC:HDL) roughly unchanged.

titles.text: Eggs raise HDL too - ratios roughly unchanged

phrasings.text: A 2025 randomized cross-over trial (61 adults, three 5-week isocaloric diets) found that a high-cholesterol diet including 2 eggs/day did not raise LDL-C relative to control, and slightly lowered it.

source.title: Impact of dietary cholesterol from eggs and saturated fat on LDL cholesterol: randomized cross-over study (Am J Clin Nutr, 2025)

titles.text: 2025 crossover RCT: eggs did not raise LDL-C (s05)

phrasings.text: Across the diets in the same 2025 randomized cross-over trial, dietary saturated-fat intake was significantly related to LDL-C, but dietary-cholesterol intake was not.

source.title: Impact of dietary cholesterol from eggs and saturated fat on LDL cholesterol: randomized cross-over study (Am J Clin Nutr, 2025)

titles.text: 2025 crossover RCT: sat fat, not cholesterol, tracked LDL-C (s05)

phrasings.text: Saturated fat, not the cholesterol in eggs, is the main driver of LDL-cholesterol elevation.

titles.text: Saturated fat, not dietary cholesterol, drives LDL (2025 crux)

phrasings.text: Dietary cholesterol meaningfully raises serum LDL cholesterol for most people.

titles.text: Dietary cholesterol meaningfully raises LDL

phrasings.text: A review argues the evidence linking dietary cholesterol to cardiovascular disease is weak, because dietary cholesterol has a limited and variable effect on serum cholesterol.

source.title: Dietary Cholesterol and the Lack of Evidence in Cardiovascular Disease (review, PMC)

titles.text: Review: dietary-cholesterol-CVD evidence is weak (s13)

phrasings.text: Dietary cholesterol has a limited and variable effect on serum cholesterol for most people.

titles.text: Dietary cholesterol's effect on serum cholesterol is limited/variable

phrasings.text: A randomized controlled trial reported that adding eggs to the diet did not significantly raise blood cholesterol.

source.title: Eggs do not raise blood cholesterol: results of a randomised controlled trial (Proc Nutr Soc)

titles.text: RCT: adding eggs did not significantly raise blood cholesterol (s06)

phrasings.text: A review notes individuals differ in how strongly dietary cholesterol raises serum cholesterol ('hyper-responders' vs 'hypo-responders'), complicating population-wide dietary advice.

source.title: Dietary Cholesterol and the Lack of Evidence in Cardiovascular Disease (review, PMC)

titles.text: Hyper- vs hypo-responder variation (s13)

phrasings.text: Individual variation in cholesterol response ('hyper-responder' vs 'hypo-responder') means a single population-wide dietary-cholesterol recommendation does not fit everyone.

titles.text: Hyper-/hypo-responder variation complicates universal advice

phrasings.text: A 2025 randomized cross-over trial measuring egg and saturated-fat effects on LDL-C is a small, short-term trial on surrogate lipid endpoints, not on cardiovascular events.

source.title: Impact of dietary cholesterol from eggs and saturated fat on LDL cholesterol: randomized cross-over study (Am J Clin Nutr, 2025)

titles.text: 2025 crossover RCT measures surrogate lipid endpoints, not CV events (s05)

phrasings.text: Blood-lipid measurements such as LDL, HDL, and total cholesterol in RCTs are surrogate markers, not direct measurements of cardiovascular events or mortality.

titles.text: Lipid RCT endpoints are surrogates, not hard CV outcomes

phrasings.text: Three large US prospective cohorts plus an updated dose-response meta-analysis found that moderate egg consumption, up to about one egg per day, was not associated with cardiovascular-disease risk in the general population.

source.title: Egg consumption and risk of CVD: three large prospective US cohorts + updated meta-analysis (BMJ 2020)

titles.text: US cohorts + meta: no CVD link up to ~1 egg/day

phrasings.text: A recent review of the egg-consumption literature concludes that for the general population, egg consumption is not clearly associated with cardiovascular-disease risk.

source.title: Eggs and Cardiovascular Disease Risk: An Update of Recent Evidence (review, PMC)

titles.text: Review: no clear general-population CVD link

phrasings.text: The same review notes the overall evidence base on egg consumption and cardiovascular disease is mixed and dominated by observational studies of modest effect size.

source.title: Eggs and Cardiovascular Disease Risk: An Update of Recent Evidence (review, PMC)

titles.text: Review: evidence base mixed, mostly observational

phrasings.text: A dose-response meta-analysis pooling about 55 studies (~2.7 million individuals) found each additional egg per day associated with about 7% higher all-cause mortality and about 13% higher cancer mortality.

source.title: Egg and Dietary Cholesterol Intake and Risk of All-Cause, CVD, and Cancer Mortality: dose-response meta-analysis (Frontiers in Nutrition, 2022)

titles.text: Dose-response meta: +7%/day all-cause mortality

phrasings.text: In the same dose-response meta-analysis, egg consumption was not significantly associated with cardiovascular-disease mortality specifically.

source.title: Egg and Dietary Cholesterol Intake and Risk of All-Cause, CVD, and Cancer Mortality: dose-response meta-analysis (Frontiers in Nutrition, 2022)

titles.text: Same meta-analysis: no CVD-mortality signal

phrasings.text: An updated meta-analysis of about 41 prospective cohort studies found higher dietary-cholesterol and egg consumption associated with increased overall and cardiovascular-disease mortality.

source.title: Zhao et al., Dietary Cholesterol, Serum Cholesterol, and Egg Consumption With Overall and Cause-Specific Mortality (Circulation, updated meta-analysis)

titles.text: 41-cohort meta: higher overall + CVD mortality

phrasings.text: In the same updated meta-analysis, the egg-cardiovascular-disease association was geographically heterogeneous: a significant positive association in US cohorts, a marginal association in European cohorts, and no association in Asian cohorts.

source.title: Zhao et al., Dietary Cholesterol, Serum Cholesterol, and Egg Consumption With Overall and Cause-Specific Mortality (Circulation, updated meta-analysis)

titles.text: Geographic heterogeneity: US positive, Asia null

phrasings.text: In the same updated meta-analysis, each 100 mg/day increment in dietary cholesterol was associated with higher all-cause and cancer mortality but not clearly with cardiovascular-disease mortality.

source.title: Zhao et al., Dietary Cholesterol, Serum Cholesterol, and Egg Consumption With Overall and Cause-Specific Mortality (Circulation, updated meta-analysis)

titles.text: Cholesterol dose-response: all-cause up, CVD unclear

phrasings.text: A large US population-based cohort study found higher egg and dietary-cholesterol intake associated with higher all-cause and cardiovascular-disease mortality, with the association driven substantially by cholesterol content rather than eggs per se.

source.title: Egg and cholesterol consumption and mortality in the US: population-based cohort study (PLOS Medicine, 2021)

titles.text: US cohort: mortality link driven by cholesterol content

phrasings.text: In the same cohort study, statistically substituting egg whites or other protein sources for whole eggs was associated with lower mortality.

source.title: Egg and cholesterol consumption and mortality in the US: population-based cohort study (PLOS Medicine, 2021)

titles.text: Egg-white substitution associated with lower mortality

phrasings.text: A longitudinal cohort study found that egg consumption's association with cardiovascular disease is modified by type-2-diabetes status: elevated cardiovascular-disease risk appears among people with diabetes at higher egg intake, but not clearly in the general population.

source.title: Longitudinal association between egg consumption and CVD risk: interaction with type 2 diabetes (Nutrition & Diabetes, 2018)

titles.text: T2D modifies egg-CVD association

phrasings.text: The same study characterizes the diabetes-subgroup interaction as one of the more consistent signals in the egg-consumption literature.

source.title: Longitudinal association between egg consumption and CVD risk: interaction with type 2 diabetes (Nutrition & Diabetes, 2018)

titles.text: Diabetes-subgroup signal called relatively consistent

phrasings.text: A systematic review and meta-analysis found no overall egg-cardiovascular-disease association in the general population but an increased cardiovascular-disease risk among people with diabetes eating more eggs.

source.title: Egg consumption in relation to risk of CVD and diabetes: systematic review and meta-analysis (PMC)

titles.text: Meta-analysis: null in general population, positive in diabetes

phrasings.text: In the same review, pooled estimates indicate that people with type-2 diabetes eating more than about seven eggs per week have substantially more cardiovascular-disease events, on the order of 69% more, than those eating few eggs.

source.title: Egg consumption in relation to risk of CVD and diabetes: systematic review and meta-analysis (PMC)

titles.text: T2D + >7 eggs/week: ~69% more CVD events

phrasings.text: For the general population, egg consumption up to about one egg per day is not associated with meaningfully increased cardiovascular-disease risk.

titles.text: General population: no meaningful CVD-risk increase up to ~1/day

phrasings.text: The mortality association found for egg or dietary-cholesterol intake in several cohort meta-analyses is driven mainly by all-cause and cancer mortality, not by cardiovascular-disease-specific mortality.

titles.text: Mortality signal is mainly all-cause/cancer, not CVD-specific

phrasings.text: Egg or dietary-cholesterol consumption is associated with increased cardiovascular-disease-specific mortality in some updated cohort meta-analyses, not only all-cause mortality.

titles.text: Rival: some meta-analyses do find a CVD-mortality signal

phrasings.text: The small increase in all-cause mortality associated with higher egg intake reflects a real causal effect of egg or dietary-cholesterol consumption.

titles.text: Mortality signal is a real causal effect of eggs

phrasings.text: The small increase in all-cause mortality associated with higher egg intake reflects residual confounding, for example by dietary pattern or other lifestyle factors correlated with egg consumption, rather than a causal effect of eggs.

titles.text: Mortality signal reflects residual confounding, not eggs

phrasings.text: The geographic heterogeneity of the egg-cardiovascular/mortality association (positive in US cohorts, marginal in European cohorts, null in Asian cohorts) is evidence that the association reflects confounding by dietary pattern or lifestyle rather than a direct causal effect of eggs.

titles.text: Geographic heterogeneity points to confounding

phrasings.text: The geographic heterogeneity in the egg-cardiovascular/mortality association may instead reflect real differences in egg-consumption dose, food preparation, or population susceptibility across regions, rather than purely confounding.

titles.text: Rival: heterogeneity may reflect real dose/susceptibility differences

phrasings.text: Among people with type-2 diabetes, higher egg intake, more than about seven eggs per week, is associated with meaningfully elevated cardiovascular-disease risk, even though this pattern is not clearly present in the general population.

titles.text: Diabetes subgroup: elevated CVD risk at higher egg intake

phrasings.text: The elevated cardiovascular-disease risk seen with higher egg intake in the type-2-diabetes subgroup reflects a real causal effect of eggs or dietary cholesterol on cardiovascular risk in that population.

titles.text: Diabetes-subgroup risk is a real causal effect of eggs

phrasings.text: The elevated cardiovascular-disease risk seen with higher egg intake in the type-2-diabetes subgroup reflects confounding by other diabetes-related dietary or lifestyle factors rather than a causal effect of eggs.

titles.text: Diabetes-subgroup risk reflects confounding, not eggs

phrasings.text: Prospective observational evidence on egg or dietary-cholesterol consumption and chronic-disease outcomes is mixed, with increased, decreased, and null associations all reported across studies.

source.title: Egg consumption and risk of chronic disease: an (un-)resolved issue? (Am J Clin Nutr editorial/review)

titles.text: Egg-disease evidence base: mixed observational findings (s16)

phrasings.text: No long-term randomized controlled trials of egg consumption on hard clinical endpoints -- cardiovascular events or all-cause/cardiovascular mortality -- exist.

source.title: Egg consumption and risk of chronic disease: an (un-)resolved issue? (Am J Clin Nutr editorial/review)

titles.text: No long-term RCTs on hard endpoints exist (s16)

phrasings.text: Prospective cohort estimates of the egg-consumption/chronic-disease association are limited by residual confounding -- healthy-user bias, socioeconomic factors, and broader dietary-pattern factors -- that observational statistical adjustment cannot fully remove.

source.title: Egg consumption and risk of chronic disease: an (un-)resolved issue? (Am J Clin Nutr editorial/review)

titles.text: Residual confounding limits cohort estimates (s16)

phrasings.text: A long-term double-blind randomized controlled trial of egg consumption on cardiovascular events is impractical to conduct, so the field must rely on imperfect observational evidence instead.

source.title: Egg consumption and risk of chronic disease: an (un-)resolved issue? (Am J Clin Nutr editorial/review)

titles.text: Long-term hard-endpoint RCT on eggs is impractical (s16)

phrasings.text: Because egg-consumption studies are predominantly observational, an association between egg or dietary-cholesterol intake and a health outcome does not by itself establish that egg or dietary-cholesterol consumption causes that outcome.

titles.text: Observational association does not establish causation

phrasings.text: Residual confounding that observational statistical adjustment cannot fully remove means cohort-observed associations between egg consumption and cardiovascular-disease or mortality outcomes may partly or wholly reflect confounding rather than a causal effect of eggs.

titles.text: Cohort associations may reflect unremovable residual confounding

phrasings.text: Randomized-controlled-trial evidence that eggs modestly affect LDL cholesterol, a surrogate marker, does not by itself establish eggs' effect on cardiovascular events or mortality, the clinical endpoints the question is actually about.

titles.text: Surrogate-endpoint RCT evidence does not settle clinical-endpoint effects

phrasings.text: Whether egg or dietary-cholesterol consumption causally raises cardiovascular-disease and mortality risk for most people remains genuinely unresolved by the current evidence base.

titles.text: The causal question remains genuinely unresolved

phrasings.text: The 2015-2020 Dietary Guidelines for Americans removed the prior recommendation to limit dietary cholesterol to under 300 mg/day.

source.title: Final 2015-2020 Dietary Guidelines for Americans (Health Affairs brief)

titles.text: 2015 guidelines drop the 300mg cholesterol cap

phrasings.text: The Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee for the 2015-2020 guidelines stated that cholesterol is 'not a nutrient of concern for overconsumption,' citing a lack of clear evidence for the specific 300 mg/day cap.

source.title: Final 2015-2020 Dietary Guidelines for Americans (Health Affairs brief)

titles.text: Committee: cholesterol 'not a nutrient of concern'

phrasings.text: The 2015-2020 Dietary Guidelines nonetheless advised keeping dietary cholesterol intake as low as practical, even after removing the numeric cap.

source.title: Final 2015-2020 Dietary Guidelines for Americans (Health Affairs brief)

titles.text: Guidelines retained 'as low as practical' cholesterol advice

phrasings.text: Nutrition commentary on the 2015 Dietary Guidelines noted that removing the numeric cholesterol cap while retaining softer cholesterol advice reflected genuine scientific uncertainty about a specific numeric limit.

source.title: Harvard Nutrition Source commentary on the 2015 Dietary Guidelines

titles.text: Commentary: cap removal reflects genuine uncertainty

phrasings.text: The original 300 mg/day dietary-cholesterol cap originated from mid-20th-century guidance (AHA 1968; U.S. Dietary Goals 1977) rather than from a trial establishing that specific threshold.

source.title: Harvard Nutrition Source commentary on the 2015 Dietary Guidelines

titles.text: 300mg cap's mid-20th-century origin, not a trial

phrasings.text: A US children's nutrition-survey study found egg consumption associated with greater daily intake of protein, lutein and zeaxanthin, choline, alpha-linolenic acid, and DHA, without displacing other nutrients from the diet.

source.title: Egg Consumption in US Children Associated With Greater Daily Nutrient Intakes (PMC)

titles.text: Egg consumption linked to higher nutrient intake (children's survey)

phrasings.text: Eggs are among the few concentrated dietary sources of choline, a nutrient many people under-consume.

source.title: Egg Consumption in US Children Associated With Greater Daily Nutrient Intakes (PMC)

titles.text: Eggs as a concentrated choline source

phrasings.text: A consumer-health source states eggs are a low-cost source of high-quality complete protein (about 6 g per large egg) with high satiety, useful for weight management.

source.title: Proven Health Benefits of Eggs (Healthline, consumer review)

titles.text: Consumer source: eggs' protein/satiety for weight management (blog)

phrasings.text: A consumer-health source states egg yolks contain lutein and zeaxanthin, antioxidants associated with reduced risk of cataracts and macular degeneration.

source.title: Proven Health Benefits of Eggs (Healthline, consumer review)

titles.text: Consumer source: lutein/zeaxanthin and eye-disease risk (blog)

phrasings.text: Eggs are a nutrient-dense food, supplying complete protein and concentrated amounts of several under-consumed nutrients including choline, lutein, and zeaxanthin.

titles.text: Eggs are nutrient-dense

phrasings.text: Eggs' high protein content and satiety help support effective weight management.

titles.text: Eggs support weight management via protein/satiety

phrasings.text: Lutein and zeaxanthin from egg yolks are associated with reduced risk of cataracts and age-related macular degeneration.

titles.text: Lutein/zeaxanthin and reduced eye-disease risk

phrasings.text: The 2015-2020 removal of the 300 mg/day dietary-cholesterol cap reflected an evidence-based reassessment that dietary cholesterol is not a population-level nutrient of concern.

titles.text: Cap removal = evidence-based reassessment

phrasings.text: The 2015-2020 guidelines' retained advice to keep dietary cholesterol intake 'as low as practical' despite removing the numeric cap shows the science on dietary cholesterol was not fully settled, not a clean exoneration.

titles.text: Cap removal left genuine scientific caution, not exoneration

phrasings.text: For most people, eggs' nutritional benefits (nutrient density, satiety) outweigh their modest, contested cardiovascular and mortality risk, making them a reasonable part of a healthy diet.

titles.text: Eggs' benefits outweigh their modest risk

phrasings.text: Given the genuinely unresolved causal question and the consistent type-2-diabetes subgroup risk, a precautionary approach to egg and dietary-cholesterol intake is warranted for most people.

titles.text: Precaution warranted given open causal question and diabetic-subgroup risk

phrasings.text: Egg and dietary-cholesterol consumption does not meaningfully raise cardiovascular-disease risk for most people.

titles.text: Eggs do not meaningfully raise CVD risk

phrasings.text: Eggs provide real nutritional benefits -- complete protein, choline, lutein and zeaxanthin, and satiety that supports weight management.

titles.text: Eggs provide real nutritional benefits
